items.myth: A 95% confidence interval means there is a 95% chance the true parameter lies inside this specific interval.
items.reality: The parameter is fixed; the interval is random. 95% describes how often the construction method captures θ across repeated samples—not a post-data probability for one interval.
items.example: You observe [0.81, 0.89] for accuracy. You cannot assign 95% probability to θ being inside it; you can say the method that produced it targets 95% long-run coverage.

items.myth: Confidence intervals and credible intervals are the same thing with different names.
items.reality: Credible intervals come from a posterior distribution in Bayesian inference and do support statements like P(θ ∈ interval | data). Confidence intervals use only sample data and frequentist coverage—different question, different math.
items.example: A 95% credible interval for conversion might read 'given data and prior, there is 95% posterior mass between 4.1% and 5.3%.' A 95% CI does not license that wording.

items.myth: If two models' 95% CIs for accuracy overlap, they must be statistically equivalent.
items.reality: Overlap of two separate CIs is a rough screen, not a formal test of equality. Two intervals can overlap while a direct test on the difference still rejects zero.
items.example: Model A: 0.84 ± 0.03 → [0.81, 0.87]; Model B: 0.80 ± 0.03 → [0.77, 0.83]. Intervals overlap, but a paired test on per-example differences may still show A > B.
